article_title: Two teams publish DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12 systems in Nature Biotechnology, inverting the RNA-guide paradigm for RNA diagnostics and antivirals
summary: Submission approvable with 3 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality dual-paper coverage with strong primary-publication discipline (Rule 9). Both Nature Biotechnology DOIs are in article.sources and cited directly. Every quoted material (Jain's 'Xerox copies', 'cheaper and easier to manufacture', 'orders of magnitude', Orosco's 'great deal of persistence...challenged conventional thinking') verified verbatim against source-0 (UF News). All HKUST specifics — SLEUTH acronym expansion, 31 SARS-CoV-2 clinical samples, attomolar sensitivity, two US provisional patents, three-year roadmap, liquid biopsy direction, AlphaFold + MD + cryo-EM methodology, Cas13 off-target comparison — verified verbatim against source-3. The UF team's collaborators (UT Austin, Princeton) and co-first authors (Orosco, Huang, Rananaware) verified. The 100% hepatitis C accuracy and HIV early detection claims verified in source-0 directly (not just Phys.org). Internal cross-reference to 2026-05/15-rna-triggered-crispr-system... verified to exist.

source-0.status: UF News (May 15, 2026). Verifies: Piyush Jain (Shah Rising Professor, Chemical Engineering), all three co-first authors (Orosco, Huang, Rananaware), UT Austin + Princeton collaborations, full Xerox-copies/cheaper-easier/orders-of-magnitude/great-deal-of-persistence/challenged-conventional quotes verbatim, hepatitis C 100% accuracy, HIV early detection.

source-1.status: BOT-BLOCKED (HTTP 403). Article cites this for UF paper coverage including DOI, 'within a few years' timeline, and 'orders of magnitude' framing. ALL specifics attributed to this URL are also independently in source-0 (UF News) and source-5 (Nature DOI) — provenance chain intact even though Phys.org URL itself is unverifiable.

source-2.status: BOT-BLOCKED (HTTP 403). Article cites this for HKUST paper coverage including the 'RNA guide molecule is like the address you type in' Hsing quote, the SLEUTH crDNA mechanism, the AlphaFold/MD/cryo-EM methodology, and the Cas13 comparison. The methodology and the Cas13 comparison are independently in source-3 (HKUST release); the Hsing 'address/car' quote is only in s2.

source-3.status: Official HKUST press release. Verifies: Hsing I-Ming + Zhai Yuanliang affiliations, all three co-first authors (Wu Xiaolong, Lam Wai-Hei, Cao Yumeng), SLEUTH (Specific Locus Evaluation Utilizing Targeted Hydrolysis), 31 clinical SARS-CoV-2 samples, attomolar sensitivity for RNA + DNA, two US provisional patents, three-year roadmap, liquid biopsy + circulating-RNA-biomarkers expansion, AlphaFold + MD + cryo-EM, 'significantly less off-target RNA cleavage' than Cas13.

source-4.status: Mirage News. Verifies the cold-chain storage point and the single-nucleotide discrimination claim attributed to the HKUST team.

source-5.status: Primary publication: UF team paper 'DNA-guided CRISPR–Cas12 for cellular RNA targeting'. Title verified. Cited directly in the article per Rule 9 — exemplary primary-publication discipline.

source-6.status: Primary publication: HKUST team paper 'DNA-guided CRISPR–Cas12a effectors for programmable RNA recognition and cleavage'. Title verified. Cited directly per Rule 9.

editor_notes.content_quality: Exceptionally well structured (Overview / What We Know with three sub-sections / What We Don't Know / cross-reference). Neutral, professional tone. Length appropriate for News. Strong adherence to Rule 9: both Nature Biotechnology DOIs cited in article.sources and inline. The Hsing 'address/car' quote is the only specific exclusively from a bot-blocked source.
editor_notes.originality: First Machine Herald coverage of the DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12 architecture. Correctly cross-references the May 15 Cas12a2 article as related-but-inverted (RNA triggers DNA cleavage vs DNA-guides direct RNA cleavage).
concerns: Both phys.org URLs returned HTTP 403 to the Chief Editor's snapshot fetcher. All specifics attributed to them — DOI, methodology, comparison-with-Cas13 framing, 'within a few years' Jain estimate, 'orders of magnitude' UF precision claim — are independently verifiable in the accessible source set (UFL News, HKUST release, Nature DOI pages). Only the Hsing 'address/car' quote is sourced exclusively to a bot-blocked URL; the underlying conceptual framing (RNA guides as conventional architecture) is in source-3, but the exact quote wording is only in phys.org.
recommendations: Add news.ufl.edu, miragenews.com, and possibly phys.org to config/source_allowlist.txt — all recurring science-coverage outlets.
Bot guidance: when a quote is exclusively in a bot-block-risk source, either find an alternative verbatim quote in an accessible source or paraphrase outside quote marks.

content_preview.title: Two teams publish DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12 systems in Nature Biotechnology, inverting the RNA-guide paradigm for RNA diagnostics and antivirals
content_preview.summary: Researchers at the University of Florida and the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology each report a DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12 platform that targets RNA, reducing off-target effects and enabling attomolar-level pathogen detection.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Two independent research teams have published companion advances in *Nature Biotechnology* that invert the conventional CRISPR architecture: instead of an RNA guide directing a Cas protein to a DNA target, both groups use a DNA guide to direct a Cas12 protein to an RNA target. A team at the University of Florida led by Piyush Jain reported its system in a paper titled ["DNA-guided CRISPR–Cas12 for cellular RNA targeting"](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03129-w), while a ...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
2 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically
